Paul opens this chapter with a promise that refuses easy comfort: the physical body we inhabit now—this tent—will be dismantled. There is no ambiguity here, no soft phrasing. You will die. Yet he does not lead with despair but with an anchor: God has prepared an eternal building in heaven, a permanent dwelling not built by human hands. The tension Paul feels is not between body and spirit as if the material is evil, but between the temporary and the eternal. He groans in this body not because flesh is shameful but because he longs for the consummation that God has promised. This is the Christian's peculiar hope—not escape from embodiment, but transformation into a new and incorruptible form. The Spirit dwelling in us now is the down payment, the guarantee that what God has promised, He will deliver. Everything Paul says about confidence and courage flows from this: we can face death, judgment, and the unknown future because we trust a God who has already begun the work of making us new.

What makes this promise functional in daily life is the single phrase in verse 7: "we walk by faith, not by sight." Paul is not describing a temporary period of uncertainty that will end when we arrive in heaven. He is describing the entire Christian existence. Even as we long for the day when we will see Christ face to face, we live now in the condition of faith—accepting God's Word about reality when our eyes cannot verify it. This walk by faith has immediate, radical consequences. Because we believe we will stand before Christ's judgment seat and answer for what we have done in this body, we aspire to please Him whether we live or die. Because we are convinced that Christ died for all and rose again, we can no longer regard anyone—or ourselves—according to the flesh, according to external appearance or earthly status. A new creation has come. The power of this realization should stop us: if you are in Christ, the old you has genuinely passed away. Not your mistakes, not your history, but the power of the old nature to define you. You are a new creation. Live like it.

The implications expand outward in the closing verses with stunning intensity. God has given us the ministry and message of reconciliation—not to hoard for ourselves but to announce to the world. We are ambassadors, which means we speak not on our own authority but on behalf of Christ Himself. When we plead with people to be reconciled to God, it is God making His appeal through us. And the foundation of everything we say is verse 21, the most compact summary of the gospel: God made the One who knew no sin to be sin on our behalf, so that in Him we might become the righteousness of God. Jesus, who was sinless, was treated as if He were sin itself so that we, who are sinful, might be treated as if we were righteous. This is not a transaction we understand; it is a mystery we accept. It is also the only reason any of us can stand before the judgment seat of Christ with confidence rather than terror. If you are in Christ, His righteousness has become your righteousness. That is not encouragement for the moment; that is the foundation for everything—how you live, what you say, who you are willing to become.
